runs.text: THE LEADERSHIP GAPWhat Gets Between You and Your GreatnessBy Lolly Daskal

plain: After decades of coaching powerful executives around the world, Lolly Daskal has observed that leaders rise to their positions relying on a specific set of values and traits. But over time, every executive reaches a point when performance declines and failure persists. Very few leaders understand why this happens or how to prevent it.

runs.text: After decades of coaching powerful executives around the world, Lolly Daskal has observed that leaders rise to their positions relying on a specific set of values and traits. But over time, every executive reaches a point when performance declines and failure persists. Very few leaders understand why this happens or how to prevent it.

plain: About the Author

Lolly Daskal is one of the most sought-after executive leadership coaches in the world. Her cross-cultural expertise spans 14 countries, six languages, and hundreds of companies. She is the Founder and CEO of Lead From Within. Her leadership program is designed to help leaders enhance performance and make a meaningful difference in their companies, their lives, and the world.

PORTERâ€™S GENERIC COMPETITIVE STRATEGIES
Choosing the road ahead

Porterâ€™s Generic Competitive Strategies describe how a company develops competitive advantage across its chosen market. There are three generic strategies: cost leadership, differentiation and focus.

plain: Overview

A company chooses to pursue one of two types of competitive advantage: either with lower costs than its competitors, or by differentiating itself along dimensions valued by customers so it can command a higher price. A company also chooses one of two types of scope: either focus (offering its products to selected segments of the market) or industry-wide, offering its product across many market segments. The generic strategy reflects choices made about both the type of competitive advantage and the scope. The concept was first described by Michael Porter in 1980.

The generic strategy reflects choices made about both the type of competitive advantage and the scope. The concept was first described by Michael Porter in 1980.

plain: Cost leadership

The strategic aim is to offer competitive prices by reducing costs and to also use lower costs to raise profit margins, fund discount campaigns, or launch an aggressive price war to gain market share and eliminate the competition. Reducing costs can also open up new markets that were less able to sustain higher prices. Another advantage of lowering costs is providing flexibility should suppliers raise prices unexpectedly and suddenly, without you also having to raise prices.

plain: Focus

plain: Understanding creativity

Creativity solves problems, challenges existing methods, and provides a better and constantly improving way forward. Given the reward, companies need to know how best to harness creativity in a way that is useful. A major flaw in traditional brainstorming is that it assumes that, if you give people the freedom to express themselves, they will magically become creative. This is not the case. For organizations, useful creativity needs to be a formal activity that requires thinking that provokes and challenges a current situation and then searches for answers.

plain: Created by Edward de Bono, the Six Thinking Hats technique explains the different styles of thinking we use while making decisions.

plain: Overview

People usually have a preferred thinking style. While this style may be useful, it can limit thinking and cause us to miss better solutions. The Six Thinking Hats method allows us to deliberately switch thinking styles so that problems are viewed from multiple perspectives. This leads to better, more balanced decisions.

plain: Using only one approach, such as logical thinking, may ignore emotional reactions, risks, or creative possibilities. Applying multiple hats gives a fuller picture and improves both decision-making and implementation.

plain: Introduction to Public SpeakingPublic speaking can be a daunting task for many. The thought of standing in front of an audience, sharing your ideas, and hoping to engage them can be nerve-wracking. However, mastering this skill is essential in both personal and professional life. In this blog post, we will explore how short videos and practical exercises can help you become a confident and effective public speaker.Public speaking is not just about delivering a speech. It involves connecting with your audience, conveying your message clearly, and leaving a lasting impression. Fortunately, with the right tools and techniques, anyone can improve their public speaking skills.

plain: Organize Your ContentStructure your speech with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion.

plain: Practice, Practice, PracticeRehearse your speech multiple times. The more you practice, the more comfortable you will become.

plain: By following these tips, you can enhance your public speaking skills and make a lasting impression on your audience.

plain: Putting It All TogetherMastering public speaking takes time and practice. By utilizing short videos and engaging in practical exercises, you can develop your skills effectively.Remember to focus on your audience, organize your content, and practice regularly. With dedication and effort, you can become a confident and effective public speaker.Practicing public speaking in front of a mirror can boost confidence and improve skills.As you embark on your public speaking journey, keep in mind that everyone starts somewhere. Embrace the learning process and celebrate your progress.Public speaking is not just about delivering a message; it is about connecting with others and sharing your unique voice. So, take a deep breath, step onto that stage, and let your voice be heard!

Hence the steps to be followed are as follows:-

plain: Networking
GETTING AN INTERVIEW CALL (RULES)

â€¢ Best place to start is your mobile phone contacts and your email / Facebook / LinkedIn contacts.â€¢ Ask your friends, Family, teachers, mentors to help you.â€¢ Make a personal connection with everyone you contact.â€¢ Speak in your own voice and words.â€¢ Keep track of every contact and schedule your follow-up calls.â€¢ Save mobile no & email the first chance you get.â€¢ Walk around when you make the calls.â€¢ Describe what youâ€™re looking for in detail.â€¢ Ask for what you want specifically.â€¢ Commit to making a few calls every day.

plain: â€¢ You must be able to interact with people and help them underrstand a new way of looking at a topic or at the world as a whole.â€¢ The main function of a teacher is to prese.nt the topic in the best way so that it is easy to understand for the students depending on their level of awareness.â€¢ Over simplifying is ok but the other way is not ok.â€¢ Good teachers â€“ the take pains to prepare themselves well, they set clear and fair expectationsâ€¢ They are good motivators.â€¢ The start with the over all macro level understanding of the subject and then get to the nuts and bolts.â€¢ They always make a list of points to remember.â€¢ They have a possitive attitude, are patient and never riducule students or make fun of them.â€¢ The act as role models and have a mentoring attitude.â€¢ They find multiple ways of explaininga point and give real life examples and use training aids.â€¢ They show enthusiasm and commitment.â€¢ The use simple language and words which the students understand.â€¢ They are impartial.

plain: WEEK 3
VISIONING
Creating your future
By imagining the future you want and then translating those ideas into practical and actionable plans, you will make it happen.

runs.text: By imagining the future you want and then translating those ideas into practical and actionable plans, you will make it happen.

plain: Orienting thinking towards the future is particularly important for middle and senior managers and leaders because it provides focus, determines the companyâ€™s culture, builds resilience and adaptability and engages employees.

plain: The need

A powerful vision motivates and guides everyone at all levels in a company. People manage what is in front of them, as daily and short-term tasks understandably dominate our routine and thinking. This certainly keeps things running smoothly in the stable present but is ill suited to coping with change or taking advantage of (or creating) opportunities. Visioning liberates us from simply managing the present, achieving more of the same or being unprepared for new developments, and thus enables us to build a more successful future.

Visioning liberates us from simply managing the present, achieving more of the same or being unprepared for new developments, and thus enables us to build a more successful future.

plain: Leading a team

THE RULE OF 150

plain: This rule is about limiting the number of people at any one location to 150.

plain: Overview

The rule is based on the idea that 150 is the largest group size that people can deal with â€“ beyond that number, it is increasingly difficult to form bonds with others. If groups are larger, hierarchies, regulations and formal measures are required. However, with fewer than 150, goals can be achieved informally and people work better and are happier, more motivated and more productive.

plain: Why it works

Co-workers find socializing, teamworking, innovating, collaborating and sharing knowledge easier to achieve in groups of fewer than 150 people. By organizing operations into smaller groups, large companies can gain the benefit of smaller groups â€“ being closer, driven, entrepreneurial, supportive and productive.

plain: The rule in practice

Gore Associates, a high-tech firm, uses this rule. It has 15 plants all within 20 kilometres (12 miles) of one another, and each with fewer than 150 employees. Porterâ€™s Generic Competitive Strategies describe how a company develops competitive advantage across its chosen market. There are three generic strategies: cost leadership, differentiation and focus.

plain: Overview

A company chooses to pursue one of two types of competitive advantage: either with lower costs than its competitors, or by differentiating itself along dimensions valued by customers so it can command a higher price. A company also chooses one of two types of scope: either focus (offering its products to selected segments of the market) or industry-wide, offering its product across many market segments. The generic strategy reflects choices made about both the type of competitive advantage and the scope. The concept was first described by Michael Porter in 1980.

plain: Cost leadership

plain: Understanding creativity

Creativity solves problems, challenges existing methods, and provides a better and constantly improving way forward. Given the reward, companies need to know how best to harness creativity in a way that is useful. A major flaw in traditional brainstorming is that it assumes that, if you give people the freedom to express themselves, they will magically become creative. This is not the case. For organizations, useful creativity needs to be a formal activity that requires thinking that provokes and challenges a current situation and then searches for answers.

plain: Overview
